nativeRange.habitat: The Lantern Bass is a reef-associated species, typically found in rocky and weedy areas at depths ranging from 1 to 80 metres. It is non-migratory.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on caridean shrimp and small fish in the wild. In aquariums, generally accepts a variety of chopped meaty foods, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and flake foods.

goodWith: peaceful community fish too large to swallow
avoidWith: small fish that can be swallowed
other Serranus species unless added simultaneously

imageCandidates.notes: Hectonichus, 2161x1618. Tack-sharp reef shot showing all diagnostic features: mottled tan/orange/red body, four dark square blotches with red/yellow bars along lower side, blue-spotted anal fin, red-rimmed eye. No watermark, best available.

Bernard Picton, 2000x1330. Sharp natural in-situ shot on sand; whole fish in frame with striping, dark lateral blotches and yellow lateral line clearly visible. Clean, no watermark; slightly more muted deeper-water coloration.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson, 960x720. Vibrant sharp full lateral view showing the four dark blotches, red bars and yellow lateral line strongly. Small corner watermark; lower resolution.

summary: The Lantern Bass is a small, colorful, and generally hardy Caribbean sea bass, commonly considered suitable for beginner reef keepers who can accommodate its semi-aggressive temperament and tendency to prey on small fish and shrimp.
careNotes:
The Lantern Bass (Serranus baldwini) is generally considered one of the hardier small basslets available through specialty marine retailers. It tends to spend much of its time perching near or among rocky crevices and live rock, from which it has been known to ambush prey. As a carnivore, it has been documented eating small fish and crustaceans, so tankmates should be sized accordingly — anything small enough to swallow is at risk.

Multiple specimens have been reported to coexist if introduced simultaneously into the same aquarium, though conspecific aggression tends to increase once territories are established. Generally best kept singly or as a bonded pair.

This species exhibits a combination of simultaneous and sequential hermaphroditism. Smaller individuals have been documented functioning as simultaneous hermaphrodites (possessing both male and female reproductive organs at once), while larger individuals may lose female function over time. This is distinct from protogyny (female-first sequential change) common in wrasses and groupers.

Coloration is described as mottled black, tan, orange, and red with four dark square blotches on the lower side, each accompanied by a red bar (appearing yellowish in deeper waters).
breedingNotes: Very little has been documented about captive breeding of this species. In the wild, it has been reported to spawn pelagically and exhibits simultaneous hermaphroditism; smaller individuals may form pairs and engage in reciprocal spawning. No reliable captive-breeding accounts are present in the hobbyist literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12.0 cm TL), temperature range (22.4–28°C), native range (Western Atlantic: S. Florida to SE Brazil), taxonomy, and reproductive biology. Temperature max is higher than LiveAquaria; values averaged across both sources for final range of 22–27°C.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive-breeding success in hobbyist literature. In the wild, the species is a simultaneous hermaphrodite and spawns pelagically, releasing eggs into open water.
